passages.title: BUSINESS INNOVATION
passages.source_html:
BUSINESS INNOVATION


As new 'wonder products' are getting harder and harder to find, what should companies do to survive in today's ever more competitive markets?


A


The Oxford English Dictionary defines innovation as 'making changes to something established'. Invention, by contrast, is the act of 'coming upon or finding; discovery'. Revolutionary 'inventions' of 20th-century US firms include, for example, the safety razor, the hydraulic brake, and DNA finger-printing. However, as Adrian Slywotsky of Mercer Management Consulting says. 'In most industries, truly differentiating new-product breakthroughs are becoming increasingly rare'. And as 'blockbuster' novel products become steadily more elusive, big companies would do well to focus instead on making lots of small things better.


B


Unfortunately, big companies often have big problems with innovation.' Innovators try to change the status quo', says Bhaskar Chakravorti of the Monitor Group, 'which is why markets tend to resist them'. Innovations frequently disrupt the way companies do things (and may have been doing them for years). And it is not just markets that are nervous of innovation. Michael Hammer, co-author of 'Re-engineering the Corporation', cites the case of a PC-maker that set out to imitate Dell's famous 'Build to Order' system of computer assembly. The company found its attempts were frustrated by both its head of manufacturing, who feared for his job, and its head of marketing, who did not want to upset his existing retail outlets. So the proposal got nowhere, and Dell continued to dominate the business.


C


Clayton Christensen, a Harvard Business School Professor, describes how disruptive innovations – simpler, cheaper and more convenient products that seriously upset the status quo – often herald the rapid downfall of thriving, long-standing businesses. This, it is widely recognised, is because most such organisations are designed to grow through a series of sustaining innovations – the sort which simply improve on existing products for existing markets.


D


William Baumol, professor at New York University, argues that large companies have been learning important lessons from the history of innovation. Most have both cut back and re-directed their Research and Development spending in recent years. Innovation by big companies is now based less on the discoveries of white-coated scientists, and more on incremental improvements in the processes that constitute daily operations.


E


According to marketing specialist Vijay Vishwanath, companies also need to adjust to the ongoing fragmentation of markets. Once-uniform mass markets are breaking up into countless 'niches', in which everything has to be customised for a small group of consumers. Looking for blockbusters in such a world is a daunting task. Another problem, according to Chakravorti, lies in the marketing of innovations. Too many executives are still stuck with the strategies used to sell Kodak's first camera 120 years ago, when the product was so revolutionary that the company could forget about competition for at least a decade. Today, rival products can be rushed onto the market at speeds which would have been unimaginable in those days.


F


Companies that fail to come up with headline-hitting new products should not despair. 'There are plenty of other, albeit less glamorous, areas where innovation can take place,' says Erik Brynjolfsson of the MIT Sloan School of Management. He claims that the roots of the USA's productivity surge originate in 'a genuine revolution in how American companies are using information technology to reinvent their business processes from top to bottom'.


G


Hammer highlights several instances where firms have added considerably to their shareholder value through what he calls operational innovations – creativity in their business processes. Notable among these is the American retail giant Wal-Mart. While superficially mundane, their pioneering idea of 'cross-docking' – shifting merchandise off trucks from suppliers and straight onto trucks heading for stores – has been fundamental to the company's ability to offer low prices, the platform for its outstanding success.


H


Companies are also being encouraged to embrace strategic innovations, following the example of such firms as Southwest Airlines, a low-cost American regional carrier, whose creative strategies and shrewd timing won it a victory over its ailing competitor US Airways. The Swedish packaging company Tetra Pak also used this style of innovation, by moving away from delivering ready-made containers for customers into providing machinery and supplying dedicated materials for them to make their own packaging. This strips out many costs from the process, and also makes it very difficult for the customer to switch suppliers. Like Southwest Airlines, they achieved success through strategic innovations alone, with little innovation in either the underlying technology or the product sold.


I


In his book 'How to Grow When Markets Don't', Slywotsky and his co-author Richard Wise recommend what they call demand innovations. A few far-sighted organisations such as the French industrial gas company Air Liquide have found success not – as in the case of strategic innovations – by meeting existing demand in a new way, but by discovering new forms of demand and adapting to supply the products or services required. Air Liquide had been a leading supplier of industrial gases until the early 1990s, when gas became a commodity and their operational income plunged. Realising the value of the skills they had gained over the decades, the company became a supplier not only of gas, but also of the management services to accompany it. Within a short time, their profits rose again.


J


There are certain things that manager can do to make innovations like these happen within their organisations. For example, projects with potential should be rapidly hived off into independent business units, away from the smothering influence of the status quo. The ultimate outcome of any one innovation may still be unpredictable, the process from which it emerges is not.

groups.title: Questions 27-40
groups.source_html:
Questions 27 - 31


Choose YES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer, choose NO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer, or choose NOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this.

27

In order to stay in the market, companies today need to concentrate their efforts on finding genuinely original products.

正确答案： B. NO

定位关键词：

companies; original products


原文：

A段，“In most industries, truly differentiating new-product breakthroughs are becoming increasingly rare'. And as 'blockbuster' novel products become steadily more elusive, big companies would do well to focus instead on making lots of small things better.”



解析：

原文中提到真正有差异化的新产品突破正变得越来越罕见，大公司最好转而专注于把许多小事情做得更好，而题目中公司应该集中精力寻找真正的原创产品。

28

Disruptive innovations often pose serious threats to even successful, well-established companies.

正确答案： A. YES

定位关键词：

disruptive innovations；threats；companies


原文：

C段，“Clayton Christensen, a Harvard Business School Professor, describes how disruptive innovations simpler, cheaper and more convenient products that seriously upset the status quo – often herald the rapid downfall of thriving, long-standing businesses.”


解析：

根据此处原文也能得出颠覆性创新往往预示着蓬勃发展的长期企业的迅速衰落。

29

A key stage in the development of sustaining innovations involves research into the strengths and weaknesses of competitors' products.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

定位关键词：

key stage；strengths and weaknesses of competitors' products


原文：

D段，“William Baumol, professor at New York University, argues that large companies have been learning important lessons from the history of innovation. Most have both cut back and re-directed their Research and Development spending in recent years. Innovation by big companies is now based less on the discoveries of white-coated scientists, and more on incremental improvements in the processes that constitute daily operations.”



解析：

题目中持续创新发展的关键阶段包括研究竞争对手产品的优势和劣势，但原文并未提到这一点。

30

Manufacturers today are able to compete with innovations over shorter time periods than in the past.

正确答案： A. YES

定位关键词：

manufacturers；shorter time periods


原文：

E段，“Too many executives are still stuck with the strategies used to sell Kodak's first camera 120 years ago, when the product was so revolutionary that the company could forget about competition for at least a decade. Today, rival products can be rushed onto the market at speeds which would have been unimaginable in those days.”


解析：

题目指出现在的制造商能够在比过去更短的时间内与创新竞争，原文引用了Kodak的例子，描述了现在竞争对手的产品在以当时难以想象的速度涌入市场。

31

It is hard to tell what the long-term result of any innovation might be.

正确答案： A. YES

定位关键词：

long-term result


原文：

J段，“The ultimate outcome of any one innovation may still be unpredictable”


解析：

原文最后一段指出任何一项创新的最终结果可能仍然是不可预测的。



同义替换：

unpredictable=hard to tell

Questions 32 - 35


Choose the correct person (A – E) for each point. 


List of People

A       Bhaskar Chakravorti

B       Michael Hammer

C       WiLlam Baumol

D       Vijay Vishwanath

E       Erik Brynjolfsson

Options (Word Bank):
A. A Bhaskar Chakravorti
B. B Michael Hammer
C. C WiLlam Baumol
D. D Vijay Vishwanath
E. E Erik Brynjolfsson
Question / ParagraphABCDE
32

32 The need for companies to cater for ever more specialised sets of customers.
✅ 正确答案：D

定位关键词：

specialised sets of customers


原文：

E段，“According to marketing specialist Vijay Vishwanath, companies also need to adjust to the ongoing fragmentation of markets. Once-uniform mass markets are breaking up into countless 'niches', in which everything has to be customised for a small group of consumers.”


解析：

原文Vijay Vishwanath 表示企业需要适应不断分化的市场，所有东西都要为一小群消费者量身定制，表明公司需要迎合越来越专业化的产品客户。


同义替换：

be customised for a small group of consumers=cater for ever more specialised sets of customers
33

33 An explanation for the recent upward trend in the US economy.
✅ 正确答案：E

定位关键词：

upward trend ；US economy


原文：

F段，“Erik Brynjolfsson of the MIT Sloan School of Management. He claims that the roots of the USA's productivity surge originate in a genuine revolution in how American companies are using information technology to reinvent their business processes from top to bottom'.”


解析：

原文Erik Brynjolfsson解释说美国生产力激增（productivity surge）的根源在于一场真正的革命，即美国公司如何利用信息技术从上到下重塑其业务流程。其中，productivity即与economy紧密挂钩。


同义替换：

surge=upward trend
34

34 An example of company personnel who resisted innovation.
✅ 正确答案：B

定位关键词：

company personnel； resisted innovation


原文：

B段，“Michael Hammer, co-author of 'Re-engineering the Corporation', cites the case of a PC-maker that set out to imitate Dell's famous 'Build to Order' system of computer assembly. The company found its attempts were frustrated by both its head of manufacturing, who feared for his job, and its head of marketing, who did not want to upset his existing retail outlets.”


解析：

原文中Michael Hammer举了一家个人电脑制造商的例子，在创新过程中受到了制造主管和营销主管的阻碍。销售主管和营销主管即为题干中的公司员工（company personnel），公司创新遭到其反对。
35

35 A shift in the way many companies are organising their budgets.
✅ 正确答案：C

定位关键词：

a shift in the way；budgets


原文：

D段，“William Baumol, professor at New York University, argues that large companies have been learning important lessons from the history of innovation. Most have both cut back and re-directed their Research and Development spending in recent years.”


解析：

原文William Baum认为大公司一直在从创新的历史中吸取重要教训，很多都削减并重新调整了研发支出，即它们在预算投入上发生了改变，a shift in the way 表示方式的改变。


同义替换：

a shift in the way=re-direct

research and development spending=budget

Questions 36 - 40


Complete the table below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS in each gap.

36
Type of innovationsDefinitionExampleCompany
operational
innovations		Being innovation in the
way existing business

are carried out

Moving goods by
system known as
Wal-Mart

innovations

Developing new
approaches to meeting
needs of existing
markets		Giving customers
means to manufacture
products themselves		Tetra Pak

innovations

Identifying and meeting
new types of customer
needs		Offering customers

to go with the

main products		Air Liquide


正确答案： 36 processes  |  37 cross-docking  |  38 strategic  |  39 demand  |  40 management services##services
Question 36

定位关键词：

operational innovation


原文：

G段，“Hammer highlights several instances where firms have added considerably to their shareholder value through what he calls operational innovations-creativity in their business processes. ”


解析：

根据operational innovation定位，可知该名词的定义即为破折号后面的表述，运营创新就是业务流程中的创造力。


同义替换：

being innovation=creativity

Question 37

定位关键词：

operational innovation


原文：

G段，“Hammer highlights several instances where firms have added considerably to their shareholder value through what he calls operational innovations-creativity in their business processes. ”


解析：

根据operational innovation定位，可知该名词的定义即为破折号后面的表述，运营创新就是业务流程中的创造力。


同义替换：

being innovation=creativity

Question 38

定位关键词：

innovations


原文：

H段，“Companies are also being encouraged to embrace strategic innovations, following the example of such firms as Southwest Airlines, a low-cost American regional carrier, whose creative strategies and shrewd timing won it a victory over its ailing competitor US Airways. The Swedish packaging company Tetra Pak also used this style of innovation, by moving away from delivering ready-made containers for customers into providing machinery and supplying dedicated materials for them to make their own packaging.”


解析：

此空格填innovation的其中一种类型，按原文顺序往下阅读可知，第二种类型便是H段提到的strategic innovations。

Question 39

定位关键词：

innovations


原文：

I段，“In his book ' How to Grow When Markets Don't ', Slywotsky and his co-author Richard Wise recommend what they call demand innovations. A few far-sighted organisations such as the French industrial gas company Air Liquide have found success not – as in the case of strategic innovations – by meeting existing demand in a new way, but by discovering new forms of demand and adapting to supply the products or services required. ”


解析：

此空格填innovation的最后一种类型，按原文顺序往下阅读可知，第三种类型便是I段提到的demand innovations。

Question 40

定位关键词：

customers；main products


原文：

I段，“ Air Liquide had been a leading supplier of industrial gases until the early 1990s, when gas became a commodity and their operational income plunged. Realising the value of the skills they had gained over the decades, the company became a supplier not only of gas, but also of the management services to accompany it. Within a short time, their profits rose again.”


解析：

原文举了Air Liquide这家公司不仅成为天然气供应商，而且还提供与之配套的管理服务使利润上升的例子，所以在这一空格内，Air Liquide不仅提供gas这一主要的商品，还提供了management services。


同义替换：

to accompany it=to go with
